text: The surgeon begins dissecting the area of the hernia sac in the mid portion of the surgical field, using a retro-rectus approach.

The surgeon has completed the dissection, revealing a large peritoneal gap with no hernia sac present. The ipsilateral posterior rectal sheath has been dissected off, and the surgeon is entering the contralateral retroactive space.

The surgeon begins cutting through the posterior rectus sheath, initially cutting only through the posterior lamella of the internal oblique. After adjusting the scissors, the surgeon successfully cuts through both the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis layers.

The surgeon cuts through both the posterior lamella of the anterior oblique and the transverse abdominus layers, performing a transversus abdominis release (TAR). This dissection releases the posterior rectus sheath from its attachments at the linea semilunaris, facilitating the medialization of the sheath to close the central gap and reduce tension on the flap closure.
The surgeon is in the preperitoneal space, carefully dissecting the peritoneum away from the transversalis fascia, revealing the familiar anatomical plane often encountered during a TAR procedure.
The surgeon navigates into the pre-transverse salus fascia plane in the upper abdomen, revealing the space between the transverse salus fascia and the transverse abdominis.

The surgeon completes the release of the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring closure of the central defect without significant tension. The final product shows the closed surgical site with no visible gaps.
